content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 2, 1990

Honorable Eric Seastrand
District Office
523 Higuera Street
San Luis Obispo, California 93401

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-154

Dear Assemblyman Seastrand:

This is in response to your request for assistance concerning the economic disclosure reporting responsibilities of your constituent under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Your request is one for general assistance. Accordingly, we are treating it as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

QUESTION

Is the Fair Political Practices Commission overburdening small business firms by requiring disclosure of certain financial interests when they contract with a city?

CONCLUSION

Although filing statements of economic interest may inconvenience small firms that contract with a city, the Political Reform Act requires designated employees listed in a city's conflict of interest code to file Form 730 to ensure full disclosure of financial interests which are likely to be affected by a designated employee in the course of his or her work. Disclosure of this information inhibits improper practices and protects the public.

¹ Government Code Section 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regulations Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-154
Page 2


FACTS

Mr. Jim Garing is the owner of a small business firm that
occasionally contracts for civil engineering and land surveying
services with the City of Arroyo Grande, California. According to
Mr. Garing, the Fair Political Practices Commission is out of hand
by requiring economic disclosure statements of small business
firms that contract with a city. If Mr. Garing and 10 of his
employees must file Form 730, it would cost his company
approximately $1,000 in staff time used to complete the form.
Mr. Garing has been recently informed by a city attorney that it
is only necessary for him to file Form 730, not his employees.

ANALYSIS

1. Economic disclosure ensures that improper practices are
inhibited.

As you may be aware, the Political Reform Act was originally
enacted by the people of the State of California by initiative in
1974. The economic disclosure provisions of the Act ensure that
public officials, and other designated employees, fully disclose
financial interests that could create a conflict of interest in
making decisions relating to a public entity.

The Act specifically requires every state and local agency to
adopt a conflict of interest code enumerating designated employees
with filing and disclosure obligations. (Sections 87300 and
87302.) In its conflict of interest code, a city, for example,
must designate those positions that involve the making of, or
participation in, decisions which may foreseeably affect any
financial interest. (Section 87302).

In general, all designated employees listed in a city's
conflict of interest code file Form 730 with the city as their
annual statement of economic interest. Although it is regrettable
that filing this form may inconvenience small firms that contract
with a city, please keep in mind that the local agency is required
to narrowly tailor the required disclosure to the types of
economic interests which are likely to be affected by a designated
employee in the course of his or her work.

2. Who must file.

Small firms occasionally contracting with a city are often
confused about who in the firm must file a disclosure statement.
They often assume that since a contract is with a firm, it is the
firm, not the individuals within the firm, who must file. We have
long advised that the individuals who provide the services and
make the decisions are the consultants that have reporting
responsibilities under the Act. (Koppes Advice Letter No. A-88-
408; Hayden Advice Letter, No. A-84-319, copies enclosed.)

Our File No. I-90-154
Page 3

The Commission has a regulation to assist local entities make
the necessary determinations. It provides that consultants are
the natural persons within the firm who provide, under contract,
information, advice, recommendation or counsel to a governmental
agency. (Regulation 18700, copy enclosed.)

Each consultant in a firm, therefore, may be required to file
individual disclosure statements.3 On the other hand, when a
consultant is retained to deliver a specific finished product or
just provide information upon which the governing body may act, he
or she would normally not be a "consultant" within the meaning of
the Political Reform Act. (Workman Advice Letter No. I-87-078;
Clifford Advice Letter No. A-83-103, copies enclosed.)

A contract consultant such as Mr. Garing would file a
statement of economic interest pursuant to a conflict of interest
code adopted by the city. At this point, it appears that
Mr. Garing has secured advice on this matter from a city attorney
who has determined the filing and disclosure obligations of Mr.
Garing and his employees. We hope that this letter is of
assistance to you. The enclosed advice letters which discuss the
reporting by consultants may also provide some guidance to you
about the requirements and purposes of the Act.

Should you have any further questions regarding this matter,
do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division

KED:LM:ld
Enclosures


3 The term consultant does not include, however, a person who:
(A) Conducts research and arrives at conclusions with
respect to his or her rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel independent of the control and
direction of the agency or of any agency officials, other
than formal contract monitoring; and
(B) Possesses no authority with respect to any agency
decision beyond the rendition of information, advice,
recommendation or counsel.

Regulation 18700(a)(2)(A)-(B).
